category_summary:
**OpenAI Codex** dominated the day's conversation. **Sam Altman** [declared Codex](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-d3a29b3ef89f) is having a "ChatGPT moment" (697K views), while **Greg Brockman** claimed it has [replaced his terminal](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-8e097fbe13dd) as his primary computer interface after two decades — signaling a potential paradigm shift in developer tooling.

- **Anthropic** published two notable research pieces: ["introspection adapters"](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-c812b0b6ca47) enabling LLMs to self-report learned behaviors and detect potential misalignment during training, and **BioMysteryBench** results showing **Claude** [solving biology problems](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-7830099b4aa4) that stumped human expert panels
- **Gary Marcus** [went viral arguing](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-ae4ae9a5a701) Big Tech's collective AI spend exceeds the Manhattan Project monthly, calling it the "greatest capital misallocation in history" — he also [shared a personal account](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-c9d9b5600937) of **Altman** lying under sworn testimony
- **swyx** offered [detailed technical praise](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-318ed7dd0d2d) of **DeepSeek V4**, highlighting its long-context efficiency innovations and confidence in skipping benchmark optimization
- **Ethan Mollick** [shared nuanced research](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-aaac345c8cff) showing AI-as-generic-study-tool hurts learning, but AI prompted as a tutor with teacher support yields large gains

Other notable signals: **John Carmack** discovered [dramatic GPU performance cliffs](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-2a1c5002fe20) at power-of-2 matrix boundaries, **David Ha** [presented ICASSP 2026 research](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-e4ee465ccae1) on "speak while thinking" voice AI, and **Mayo Clinic's** REDMOD AI [detected pancreatic cancer](/?date=2026-04-30&category=social#item-50f8193c8b0f) 16 months before doctors on routine CT scans.

items.title: Sheer insanity. Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Meta collectively are spending more money than the Ma...
items.content:
Sheer insanity. Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Meta collectively are spending more money than the Manhattan Project *every single month*. More than 12x the Manhattan Project every year.

And what they have got to show for it? 

None are making major profits on AI; none has a technical moat; a massive price war is inevitable. And few of their customers are seeing major returns on investment.

Greatest capital misallocation in history.

items.title: New on the Science Blog: We gave Claude 99 problems analyzing real biological data and compared its ...
items.content:
New on the Science Blog: We gave Claude 99 problems analyzing real biological data and compared its performance against an expert panel.

On 23 problems, the experts were stumped. Our most recent models solved roughly 30% of those—and most of the rest. https://t.co/BYqr76zxhk

items.title: IMO DeepSeek v4 demonstrated utter confidence and competence by not benchmaxxing, not focusing on so...
items.content:
IMO DeepSeek v4 demonstrated utter confidence and competence by not benchmaxxing, not focusing on some BS final run cost, not even spending inference-optimal compute.

just showed up, demonstrated SOTA long context efficiency techniques (CSA, HCA, mHC, flash at 8% cost of pro, which itself is 14% cost of opus), dropped the best open base models in the world, peaced out. 

BYO posttraining. leave that to the agent labs to pick up the scraps. bravo.

items.title: For years, voice AI has been stuck in a rigid loop: think, then speak. But real human conversation i...
items.content:
For years, voice AI has been stuck in a rigid loop: think, then speak. But real human conversation is messy, overlapping, and asynchronous.

In our new #ICASSP2026 work, we built a tandem architecture that shifts the paradigm to “speak while thinking.” A fast speech model starts replying instantly, while a backend LLM runs in parallel to inject deep knowledge on the fly.

It’s a completely different way to approach conversational AI, making it feel remarkably more alive.

Blog: https://t.co/qZ4cEKUUIm 🐢
items.author: @hardmaru
items.summary: Hardmaru (David Ha) presents ICASSP 2026 research on tandem architecture for voice AI - 'speak while thinking' approach where fast speech model replies instantly while LLM injects knowledge in parallel
items.reasoning: Original research from respected AI researcher presenting novel architecture for conversational AI with very high engagement (50K views). Technical contribution to voice AI field.

items.content:
Full Workshop: @OpenAI Codex masterclass

The agent is no longer just one chat window. In this workshop, @reach_vb and @kagigz get into how coding systems start to change when you can delegate work across subagents, split tasks up, and manage more context than a single thread can comfortably hold.

If you're trying to understand where the next generation of coding agents is going, this is a useful look at the shape of that future from inside @OpenAI!

Full Workshop: https://t.co/Wxnqu4cv1z

One reason I don’t think “judgment” is going to be a distinctly human role in working with AI is that the most recent agentic models have gotten quite good at some types of judgment. You can’t do the kind of high complexity, long-run tasks that current AIs can do without it.

From GPT-5.5 guide👇

items.title:
Everything I Learned Training Frontier Small Models @maximelabonne 

After a lot of hype around fron...
items.content:
Everything I Learned Training Frontier Small Models @maximelabonne 

After a lot of hype around frontier model training, Maxime gives the practical version: what actually happens when you try to train smaller models that still matter. From data quality and synthetic data to evals, distillation, and where small models still punch above their weight, this is a concise field report from someone doing the work at @liquidai.

If you're building with open models, post training, or trying to understand where the next wave of useful local-ish capability comes from, this one is worth your time.

https://t.co/BBqFj5mYbP

items.title:
AI keeps moving up the timeline on cancer detection. 

Mayo Clinic just built an AI called REDMOD th...
items.content:
AI keeps moving up the timeline on cancer detection. 

Mayo Clinic just built an AI called REDMOD that spots pancreatic cancer on routine CT scans 16 months before doctors do.

It caught 73% of early cancers. On scans more than two years before diagnosis, it was nearly 3x better.

The model reads "hundreds of quantitative imaging features", texture and structure patterns invisible to human radiologists.

The 5-year survival rate for pancreatic cancer is below 15%, making early diagnosis critical to treatment.

items.title:
AI agents rebuilt 48 papers without seeing the code or the paper. 

Most academic papers are suppose...
items.content:
AI agents rebuilt 48 papers without seeing the code or the paper. 

Most academic papers are supposed to be self-contained. 

Turns out, AI can now test that claim.

Researchers built an agentic pipeline that reads a paper's methods section, looks at the raw data, and rewrites the analysis code from scratch.

No peeking at the original code. No peeking at the results.

They ran it on 48 published economics and political science papers, all previously verified as reproducible.

The best agent matched the original sign of coefficients 91% of the time. 

Over 80% of estimates landed inside the 95% confidence interval.

Here is the twist.

When reproductions failed, the cause was usually:

> Vague variable definitions
> Missing filtering rules
> Unspecified statistical choices
> Ambiguous coding decisions

The papers themselves were the bottleneck, not the AI.

The full pipeline, prompts, and evaluation framework are open-source.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AI agents rebuilt 48 academic papers from scratch (economics/poli sci) without seeing code. Best agent matched coefficient signs 91% of time, 80%+ within 95% CI. Failures were mainly due to vague paper descriptions, not AI limitations.
items.reasoning: Important research on AI's ability to reproduce academic work, with the surprising finding that papers themselves are the bottleneck. Implications for scientific reproducibility and methodology standards. Open-source pipeline.
items.summary_html: AI agents rebuilt 48 academic papers from scratch (economics/poli sci) without seeing code. Best agent matched coefficient signs 91% of time, 80%+ within 95% CI. Failures were mainly due to vague paper descriptions, not AI limitations.

items.title:
Delegating work to AI corrupts 25% of your document in 20 edits. 

Researchers just tested if you ca...
items.content:
Delegating work to AI corrupts 25% of your document in 20 edits. 

Researchers just tested if you can actually trust AI to edit your documents.

The answer is messier than expected.

A new benchmark called DELEGATE-52 ran 19 top models through 310 real work environments across 52 fields.

Coding, music notation, crystallography, you name it.

Each task involved 5-10 complex edits chained together to simulate real workflows.

Here's what they found after 20 interactions:

> Frontier models corrupt 25% of content
> Average model loses 50% entirely
> Giving agents tools made it worse
> Python was the only reliable domain
> Best performer "ready" in 11/52 areas

The damage isn't gradual.

Models work perfectly for rounds, then catastrophically drop 10% of a file in one step.

These rare failures cause 80% of total corruption.

Bigger documents and longer sessions multiply the harm 5x.

The benchmark is now public for anyone to test.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: DELEGATE-52 benchmark shows AI corrupts 25% of documents after 20 edits, with frontier models losing content catastrophically in rare failure events
items.reasoning: Important research finding about AI reliability in real workflows. Quantifies a critical limitation of current models for document editing. Relevant to anyone using AI agents for work.
items.summary_html: DELEGATE-52 benchmark shows AI corrupts 25% of documents after 20 edits, with frontier models losing content catastrophically in rare failure events

It's an honor to support the Google for Korea event as we celebrate the 1...
items.content:
Ten years of AlphaGo!⚫️⚪️

It's an honor to support the Google for Korea event as we celebrate the 10th anniversary of the legendary AlphaGo match.

Hearing from @demishassabis and Lee Sedol firsthand was a great way to kick things off, alongside some big updates for Gemini Robotics and Gemini for Education.

In the coming days, there will be more Gemini & Gemma events with the local developer & startup community.

And lastly, @GoogleDeepMind announced a partnership with the Republic of Korea. Very exciting week in Seoul!🇰🇷

items.title:
🏆 vLLM powers the fastest inference on NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra on Artificial Analysis.

On @digitaloc...
items.content:
🏆 vLLM powers the fastest inference on NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra on Artificial Analysis.

On @digitalocean's Serverless Inference, powered by vLLM on NVIDIA HGX B300:
🥇 AA #1 output speed for DeepSeek V3.2 (230 tok/s, 0.96s TTFT) and Qwen 3.5 397B
🔧 MiniMax-M2.5: 23% TPOT gain via an EAGLE3 draft model trained on TorchSpec

Co-design highlights:
- NVFP4 quantization on Blackwell Ultra
- EAGLE3 + MTP speculative decoding
- Per-model kernel fusion

Thanks to @digitalocean, @nvidia, and @inferact for the collaboration. Optimizations land back in open-source vLLM.

🔗 https://t.co/zCnQIr5cRa
items.author: @vllm_project

items.reasoning: Important infrastructure benchmark showing state-of-the-art inference speeds on latest hardware. Specific technical details about optimization techniques. Collaboration between vLLM, DigitalOcean, NVIDIA. Decent engagement (27k views).

items.content:
The Musk-Altman trial is clearly a battle of egos—and it’s hard to root for either side—𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝘀𝗼 𝗮 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗔𝗜 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗯𝗲 𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗶𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗮 𝗻𝗼𝗻𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗳𝗶𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗵𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆, which it clearly no longer is. 

I am no fan of Musk but the world would be better off if OpenAI were forced to pursue its original mission.

items.summary_html: Marcus argues the Musk-Altman trial is fundamentally about whether OpenAI should be held to its nonprofit promises, supports OpenAI being forced to pursue original mission
items.content_html:
The Musk-Altman trial is clearly a battle of egos—and it’s hard to root for either side—𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝘀𝗼 𝗮 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗔𝗜 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗯𝗲 𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗶𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗮 𝗻𝗼𝗻𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗳𝗶𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗵𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆, which it clearly no longer is.

I am no fan of Musk but the world would be better off if OpenAI were forced to pursue its original mission.

items.title: For the past few years, humans have been doing “prompt engineering” to coax the best performance out...
items.content:
For the past few years, humans have been doing “prompt engineering” to coax the best performance out of different LLMs. In this work, we explored what happens if we train an AI to do that job instead.

Link to our #ICLR2026 paper: arxiv.org/abs/2512.04388

items.title:
Agentic coding is the ideal mechanism for enforcing TDD and being strict with it.

Here is a summary...
items.content:
Agentic coding is the ideal mechanism for enforcing TDD and being strict with it.

Here is a summary of the workflow I coded as part of my implementation skill in Claude Code:

1. Before writing any code, write a test that fails
2. Run the test and ensure it fails
3. If you get an error, fix the test until it runs but fails
4. Once the test fails, write the code
5. Run the test and ensure it succeeds
6. If the test fails, go back to step 4
7. Once the test succeeds, verify that the task is complete
8. If the task is not complete, go back to step 1

This loop forces the agent to write tests before writing any code, and tries to keep each test as simple as possible.

This is token hungry upfront, but it has many advantages:

1. Simpler code
2. More modular code
3. Fewer bugs and regressions later
4. Easier to troubleshoot
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama describes his TDD (Test-Driven Development) workflow for agentic coding in Claude Code, enforcing write-test-first loops.
items.reasoning: Practical and detailed workflow for using AI coding agents with TDD. High engagement (153 likes, 14K views). Actionable technical content about making AI coding more reliable.
items.summary_html: Santiago Valdarrama describes his TDD (Test-Driven Development) workflow for agentic coding in Claude Code, enforcing write-test-first loops.

This is token hungry upfront, but it has many advantages:

items.title: Niantic has the 3D model of the entire world. I got a demo a few months ago, it has a digital twin o...
items.content:
Niantic has the 3D model of the entire world. I got a demo a few months ago, it has a digital twin of the entire world down to about a millimeter per voxel.

As soon as a camera turns on that uses it it will know where you are and what you are looking at. 

The only variable is things moving around. 

Humans.

And for that we don't really care unless they are friends, or people we know. 

Or we are at a conference.

Then you are wearing a badge.

The glasses coming next year will read the badge and tell me about your LinkedIn posts.

And will know where we met.

I have an early version of this in my @Looki_ai pendant.

Still needs improvements, though. I spoke at the Silicon Valley AI Hub (interviewed four robotics founders). 

The report didn't have their names. 

When I asked Looki's founder about this, he said "upgrades coming soon."

Better hurry. Apple can do it a better way with Ultra Wide Band Radios.

Yeah, I'll let you see all my stuff if you meet me at a conference. 

"Hey AI in my pendant can you find a restaurant to eat at?"

I love Bilawal's videos. They show the future that's coming at us fast.

It's easy to predict the future if you can get into San Francisco R&D labs. @NianticSpatial's is in the Ferry Building. Which is a trip. To enter its headquarters you have to jump into an old phone booth. 

When you go through that to the other side it is like being in the future.

items.title: By training a Conductor model with RL, we found that it naturally learns to write highly effective, ...
items.content:
By training a Conductor model with RL, we found that it naturally learns to write highly effective, custom instructions for a whole pool of other models. It essentially learns to ‘manage’ them in natural language.

arxiv.org/abs/2512.04388

items.title:
.@MadrigalPharma  is living in the (agentic) future.

If you haven't heard of Madrigal, they are a p...
items.content:
.@MadrigalPharma  is living in the (agentic) future.

If you haven't heard of Madrigal, they are a pioneering biopharma company.

Some months ago, they set out to solve the problem of integrating, searching, and synthesizing information from diverse datasets at scale. This lead to the creation of an enterprise multi-agent platform

They wrote about their journey in a case study on our blog: https://t.co/0xzGFycbs9

My favorite part: highlighting observability as the step to close the gap between prototype and production. We see the importance of observability daily - to help give insights to debug and improve your systems. This iteration loop is a key part of building reliable agents, and I'm thrilled to share how Madrigal expertly used it

items.title:
We may experience an explosion in litigation around AI systems over the next 2 years. 

Claude is aw...
items.content:
We may experience an explosion in litigation around AI systems over the next 2 years. 

Claude is awesome in particular in speeding up coding and research as is GPT 5.5. But even the best LLMs make mistakes in production code that requires experienced  devs to detect, and plug the gaps. 

I wonder whether law firms may view whether those replacing skilled humans now and relying purely on LLMs may potentially face a genuine risk for tortious liability (negligence or recklessness) or contract breach as the models make fundamental errors if scaled without extra engineering and governance for domain specific tasks. 

Even then it is better to have human + AI for productivity gains. Neurosymbolic AI takes us there but requires extra skills and effort that many  dev teams in corporates and startups skip due to extra time (cost) or simply due to lack of knowledge. I had to learn both ANNs and Symbolic Logic as a student of AI. Many data scientists don’t. @pierrepinna @GaryMarcus
items.author: @DeepLearn007
items.summary: DeepLearn007 discussing potential litigation explosion around AI systems - LLMs make production code mistakes requiring experienced devs, potential tortious liability for those replacing skilled humans with AI. Advocates neurosymbolic AI and human+AI approach.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful analysis of emerging AI liability risks, connects technical limitations (LLM code errors) to legal consequences. Mentions neurosymbolic AI as solution. Relevant to AI governance and enterprise risk.
items.summary_html: DeepLearn007 discussing potential litigation explosion around AI systems - LLMs make production code mistakes requiring experienced devs, potential tortious liability for those replacing skilled humans with AI. Advocates neurosymbolic AI and human+AI approach.
items.content_html:
We may experience an explosion in litigation around AI systems over the next 2 years.

Claude is awesome in particular in speeding up coding and research as is GPT 5.5. But even the best LLMs make mistakes in production code that requires experienced devs to detect, and plug the gaps.

I wonder whether law firms may view whether those replacing skilled humans now and relying purely on LLMs may potentially face a genuine risk for tortious liability (negligence or recklessness) or contract breach as the models make fundamental errors if scaled without extra engineering and governance for domain specific tasks.

Even then it is better to have human + AI for productivity gains. Neurosymbolic AI takes us there but requires extra skills and effort that many dev teams in corporates and startups skip due to extra time (cost) or simply due to lack of knowledge. I had to learn both ANNs and Symbolic Logic as a student of AI. Many data scientists don’t. @pierrepinna @GaryMarcus

items.title:
日経クロステックの連載記事「R&Dを根底から変革、普及始まる『AI科学者』」にて、Sakana AIのResearch Scientist、Robert Langeへの取材記事が掲載されました。

当...
items.content:
日経クロステックの連載記事「R&Dを根底から変革、普及始まる『AI科学者』」にて、Sakana AIのResearch Scientist、Robert Langeへの取材記事が掲載されました。

当社のAIサイエンティストについて、テーマ探索から論文査読まで研究の全工程を自動的に遂行する仕組みと、その現状の到達点・限界について解説されています。

記事でも触れていただいているとおり、AIサイエンティストに関する論文は2026年3月に学術誌Natureに掲載されました。基盤となるAIモデルの性能向上に伴って生成される論文の質が改善されうることを実験的に示せた点は、本研究の重要な成果の一つです。

items.title:
Scientists just built a robot smaller than the width of a human hair (!)

It has no motor, no comput...
items.content:
Scientists just built a robot smaller than the width of a human hair (!)

It has no motor, no computer and no battery

It's a 3D-printed, flexible chain of microscopic segments that, after being hit with an electric field, started swimming through liquid like a snake.

Try to block its path? It twists around the obstacle, as if it can sense what's in its way.

But the strangest thing about it all is that the behavior isn't programmed. It emerges from the physics of the structure itself.

The entire robot is 5 micrometers long (for context: a human hair is 70).

The researchers think this could one day deliver drugs directly to tumors or infections inside the human body.

Sometimes the simplest designs produce the most lifelike behavior.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Scientists built a 5-micrometer snake-like robot (smaller than hair width) that swims through liquid and navigates obstacles without programming — behavior emerges from physics of the structure itself.

items.title:
In talking to lots of people in tech we are all burning out.

Why?

Those of us inside the AI bubble...
items.content:
In talking to lots of people in tech we are all burning out.

Why?

Those of us inside the AI bubble are staying up all night to play with our agents.

Those outside the AI bubble are living in fear and disgust.

I'm burned out. My agents are always asking me to do things.

As to toxic jobs.

They always were toxic, but now the toxicity is getting turned up to 11. 

You are right. But there aren't enough therapists. So most of us will end up talking to our AI about our mental health. 

What a world. 

Go touch grass. Your agents will wait. :-)

items.title:
KAIKAKU AI just trained an AI to taste.

Their team fed the model nothing but recipes from existing ...
items.content:
KAIKAKU AI just trained an AI to taste.

Their team fed the model nothing but recipes from existing cookbooks. No nutrition data or chemistry.

The model worked out what was sweet, salty, spicy, and bitter from ingredient pairings alone.

It even picked up texture (chewy vs. crunchy) and which cuisine each ingredient belonged to.

Co-founder Josef Chen calls it food AI's ChatGPT moment:

https://t.co/hZBuu5FXeq

items.title: Most models point by generating text coordinates. MolmoPoint points directly, making it less brittle...
items.content:
Most models point by generating text coordinates. MolmoPoint points directly, making it less brittle than other systems.

As Chris puts it, when the team asked how a model could point across modalities, “pointing directly to input [data] was the obvious answer.”

items.summary: MolmoPoint generates direct pixel-level pointing rather than text coordinates, making it less brittle
items.reasoning: Technical innovation in visual grounding - direct pointing vs text coordinate generation
items.summary_html: MolmoPoint generates direct pixel-level pointing rather than text coordinates, making it less brittle
items.content_html:
Most models point by generating text coordinates. MolmoPoint points directly, making it less brittle than other systems.

As Chris puts it, when the team asked how a model could point across modalities, “pointing directly to input [data] was the obvious answer.”

items.title:
I gotta do a new review of glasses.

My favorites at the moment: 
Heavyweights: Apple Vision Pro
Mid...
items.content:
I gotta do a new review of glasses.

My favorites at the moment: 
Heavyweights: Apple Vision Pro
Middleweights: Viture
Lightweights: Mentra Glass or Even Realities

The Viture is a great set to sit down and compute virtually. Like when you are stuck in an airplane, or want to sit down at a coffee shop to work. 

Beautiful screen inside. 

The lightweight division is interesting. The Even Realities have small green and black screens, so your AI agents can talk to you with text. The Mentras (or Meta Raybans) have cameras, microphones, and speaker). 

What I want is an Apple Vision Pro that's as light as the Even Realities. That is years away. The Viture doesn't do as much magic stuff (VR/AR/XR kinds of 3D computing) as the Apple Vision Pro, but most people just want to work on a big virtual screen right now and they are amazing at doing that.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.reasoning: Comprehensive overview of AR glasses market positioning with practical insights. Good engagement and useful categorization.

What I want is an Apple Vision Pro that's as light as the Even Realities. That is years away. The Viture doesn't do as much magic stuff (VR/AR/XR kinds of 3D computing) as the Apple Vision Pro, but most people just want to work on a big virtual screen right now and they are amazing at doing that.

items.title:
Autonomous B2B Agents-as-a-Service

Entire business functions collapse into leased agent fleets: pro...
items.content:
Autonomous B2B Agents-as-a-Service

Entire business functions collapse into leased agent fleets: procurement, finance ops, legal workflows, even internal strategy. The shift is from SaaS (tools) to AaaS (outcomes), where companies pay for completed work, not software seats.

Second-order effect: org charts flatten and vendors become “shadow departments.” The enduring behavior is that companies optimize for efficiency and control, but now control comes from orchestration, not ownership of labor.

Personal AI Cooperative Networks

Individuals organize into AI-coordinated micro-coops for buying po...
items.content:
Personal AI Cooperative Networks

Individuals organize into AI-coordinated micro-coops for buying power, services, and income generation. These systems negotiate on behalf of groups, allocate work, and distribute value transparently.

The shift is from gig platforms (extractive, centralized) to coordinated collectives (aligned, bottom-up). Humans have always formed groups for leverage; AI removes coordination friction.

items.title:
AI Agent Compliance & Governance Layer

Autonomous compliance agents evolve from tools into always-o...
items.content:
AI Agent Compliance & Governance Layer

Autonomous compliance agents evolve from tools into always-on governance infrastructure. As regulation accelerates (AI, ESG, cross-border data, tax), the bottleneck shifts from interpretation to continuous enforcement and board-level visibility.

These agents don’t just flag risk, they simulate decisions, propose compliant paths, and log everything as audit-ready memory. The “why” is simple: complexity compounds faster than headcount, and liability increasingly sits with executives who need real-time assurance. This becomes as core as ERP, but decision-aware.

items.reasoning: Practical and timely given increasing AI regulation. Decent engagement.

items.title: Improving Transformers with Self Attention Mechanism- this technique is at the heart of GPT and most...
items.content: Improving Transformers with Self Attention Mechanism- this technique is at the heart of GPT and most LLMs. I would not imply that this issue is their biggest problem though. That said no one has to date managed to replace them with a viable alternative. Maybe that changes but since their inception in 2017 and in particular last few years they have transformed the AI space. We  can improve them with hybrid neurosymbolic Ai.

items.title:
AI-Generated IP & Virtual Asset Licensors

Mass-scale generation of characters, designs, music, and ...
items.content:
AI-Generated IP & Virtual Asset Licensors

Mass-scale generation of characters, designs, music, and worlds becomes a licensable asset class. Instead of a few blockbuster IPs, there are millions of niche, dynamically evolving ones.

The key shift is from scarcity-driven IP to abundance-managed IP. Value comes from curation, distribution, and network effects, not just creation.

items.title:
AI-Residual Economies (Data → Income)

Users begin to expect compensation for their contribution to ...
items.content:
AI-Residual Economies (Data → Income)

Users begin to expect compensation for their contribution to AI systems, not just free usage. These platforms track, attribute, and pay micro-royalties when user data, behavior, or feedback improves models.

The deeper shift is psychological: people move from “users” to “participants” in economic systems. This taps into fairness instincts that have always existed but were previously unenforceable.

items.title:
AI Reputation & Trust Infrastructure

A persistent, portable reputation layer becomes a core primiti...
items.content:
AI Reputation & Trust Infrastructure

A persistent, portable reputation layer becomes a core primitive for transactions. Instead of fragmented trust (LinkedIn, Uber, credit scores), AI aggregates behavior into a dynamic trust graph usable across platforms.

This enables undercollateralized lending, autonomous contracting, and agent-to-agent transactions. Trust has always been foundational, but now it becomes machine-readable and liquid.

items.content:
Temporal Reflection & Decision Simulation Systems

Agents simulate future scenarios across personal, corporate, and societal decisions. Instead of static analytics, users interact with branching timelines and probabilistic outcomes.

This taps into a timeless behavior: humans constantly imagine “what if,” but now that process is externalized and systematized. Decision-making becomes less about intuition and more about navigating modeled futures.

items.title:
AI-Enabled Wellbeing & Social Fabric Systems

Mental health expands into broader “life infrastructur...
items.content:
AI-Enabled Wellbeing & Social Fabric Systems

Mental health expands into broader “life infrastructure” where AI connects people to communities, support systems, and purpose-driven activity. Instead of isolated therapy apps, this becomes a networked system of belonging.

The second-order effect: loneliness becomes a solvable coordination problem, not just a psychological one. This aligns with a constant: humans need connection, but modern systems have eroded it.

items.title:
Apple is pushing Formula 1: 

Glasses for watching Formula 1 in a much better way are possible but a...
items.content:
Apple is pushing Formula 1: 

Glasses for watching Formula 1 in a much better way are possible but aren’t cheap enough to manufacture I hear. 

So we wait.

My brother in law worked at Apple as an exec while I worked at Microsoft back in the mid 2000’s. Steve Jobs killed the iPhone project several times.

Apple will do it when they can make it great.

Right now it can’t. Too heavy. Too expensive.

And I love mine.

items.title:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/pAKXiKmOTp

A Practical Guide to Reinforceme...
items.content:
New release from @PacktDataML available at https://t.co/pAKXiKmOTp

A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).

Amazon Summary:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.

Trust-Based Micro-Community Networks

Small, high-trust groups become more valuable than large, low-...
items.content:
Trust-Based Micro-Community Networks

Small, high-trust groups become more valuable than large, low-trust networks. AI helps form, maintain, and govern these groups based on shared values and behavior.

This is a direct reaction to the dilution of trust in large platforms. Humans naturally organize into tribes; this formalizes and optimizes that instinct.

The enduring truth: people learn differently, but systems have historically ignored that. AI makes personalization scalable and continuous.

Narrative Economies & Co-Creation Platforms

Stories evolve from static content into living systems co-created with audiences. Users don’t just consume IP, they shape it, own parts of it, and participate in its evolution. 

The business model shifts from distribution to participation. Humans have always been drawn to stories; now they become stakeholders in them.

Identity Orchestration Platforms

Digital identity fragments across contexts, platforms, and now AI-generated personas. These platforms reconcile, manage, and even strategically deploy multiple identities. 

The deeper driver is cognitive load: people want coherence, but also flexibility in how they present themselves. As identity becomes programmable, managing it becomes a service.

items.title:
The insight that stood out:

HR decisions depend on layered context.

Not just general rules, but pr...
items.content:
The insight that stood out:

HR decisions depend on layered context.

Not just general rules, but priorities.

For example:

→ National legislation sets the baseline
→ Company policy often overrides in practice
→ Edge cases change everything

If your AI is not trained on this, it will give the wrong answers confidently.

items.title:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not ...
items.content:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not transformation.
That is a slightly smarter auto-reply.

Real conversational AI needs 3 things:

→ context from the customer history
→ access to business systems
→ the ability to take action in real time

If it cannot do something, it is still just a chatbot.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues real conversational AI needs context, system access, and real-time action capability
items.reasoning: Makes a valid technical point about AI chatbot requirements (context, system integration, action capability) but part of sponsored content series
items.summary_html: Argues real conversational AI needs context, system access, and real-time action capability
items.content_html:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not transformation.

That is a slightly smarter auto-reply.

Real conversational AI needs 3 things:

→ context from the customer history

→ access to business systems

→ the ability to take action in real time

If it cannot do something, it is still just a chatbot.

items.title: 🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and Data Science Competitions with Machine Learning, GenAI, ...
items.content:
🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and Data Science Competitions with Machine Learning, GenAI, and LLMs [2nd Ed.]: https://t.co/ZjNKsvFl06 v/ @PacktDataML

Table of Contents:
🔶Introducing Data Science Competition
🔷Organizing Data with Datasets
🔶Work & Learn with Kaggle Notebooks
🔷Kaggle Models
🔶Leveraging Discussion Forums
🔷Detailing Competition Tasks & Metrics
🔶Designing Good Validation Schemes
🔷Modeling for Tabular Competitions
🔶Hyperparameter Optimization
🔷Ensembling & Stacking Solutions
🔶Modeling Image Classification & Segmentation

My Review (on Amazon):
This 700-page masterpiece of writing covers everything you need—start to finish—to be a competitive coder, specifically for Kaggle data science competitions. The book covers the mechanics of the competitions (platform, resources, rankings, leaderboards), then the infrastructure (notebooks, GitHub, data sets, frameworks, discussion forums), and then nearly 500 pages devoted to "Elevating Your Game" (in-depth coverage of modeling techniques, evaluation metrics, validation strategies, hyperparameter optimization, ensembles, stacking, and various categories of competitions: tabular data, computer vision, NLP, Gen AI, simulations). The book concludes with a valuable section on building your Kaggle portfolio for career advancement and new opportunities. This is an outstanding data science / AI / Machine Learning training resource for anyone, even if you are not into the competitions, though especially if you are a dedicated Kaggler.

items.title:
Resource Orchestration & Redistribution Systems

As production becomes more efficient (energy, goods...
items.content:
Resource Orchestration & Redistribution Systems

As production becomes more efficient (energy, goods, services), the constraint shifts to allocation. These systems dynamically route resources based on need, efficiency, and policy.

Whether or not we reach “post-scarcity,” distribution becomes the central problem. This is a systems-level evolution of logistics and markets.

items.title:
Anthropocene Mediation Platforms (Human ↔ Nature)

Systems represent non-human stakeholders (rivers,...
items.content:
Anthropocene Mediation Platforms (Human ↔ Nature)

Systems represent non-human stakeholders (rivers, forests, ecosystems) in decision-making processes. AI models impact, advocates for outcomes, and participates in governance frameworks.

This emerges from a shift where environmental externalities can no longer be ignored. It formalizes something humans intuitively understand but haven’t operationalized.

items.title:
Cultural Memory & Legacy Systems

Agents preserve languages, traditions, and personal histories as l...
items.content:
Cultural Memory & Legacy Systems

Agents preserve languages, traditions, and personal histories as living, interactive systems. This extends to individuals (family memory) and entire cultures.

The driver is loss: as the world accelerates, preservation becomes more valuable. Humans have always sought legacy; this makes it dynamic and accessible.

items.title:
Ethical & Moral Decision Support Systems

AI systems help individuals and organizations navigate com...
items.content:
Ethical & Moral Decision Support Systems

AI systems help individuals and organizations navigate complex ethical decisions. These aren’t prescriptive, but reflective, offering frameworks, precedent analysis, and consequence modeling.

As decisions become more complex and visible, people seek external validation for moral reasoning. This becomes a “cognitive advisor” layer.

items.title:
HR teams are navigating an overload of AI tools.

The real problem is not access, it is clarity.

Mo...
items.content:
HR teams are navigating an overload of AI tools.

The real problem is not access, it is clarity.

Most teams don’t define:

→ What problems AI should solve
→ What it should not touch
→ How decisions are actually made internally

Without that, even the best tools underperform.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.content_html:
HR teams are navigating an overload of AI tools.

The real problem is not access, it is clarity.

Most teams don’t define:

→ What problems AI should solve

→ What it should not touch

→ How decisions are actually made internally

Without that, even the best tools underperform.

items.title:
My biggest takeaway from this conversation:

The funnel is not disappearing, but it is collapsing in...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway from this conversation:

The funnel is not disappearing, but it is collapsing into a single interaction.

Awareness, consideration, decision, all happening in one chat, in seconds.

That is a very different operating model for marketing, sales, and service.

Watch the full video to see how this shift is playing out in practice, and how Spoki is thinking about conversational experiences that turn messages into outcomes.

What would need to change in your business for a conversation to become your primary interface with customers?

Clumio (a...
items.content:
𝗜𝗖𝗬𝗠𝗜: look at this important announcement from @Commvault last week at #GoogleCloudNext…

Clumio (a #Commvault company) Extends Its Rapid Recovery and Resilience Capabilities to Google Cloud:
https://t.co/GemtpYOJP5

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗯𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗳𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗚𝗼𝗼𝗴𝗹𝗲 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗲𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗖𝗹𝘂𝗺𝗶𝗼:
Every AI model is only as good as the data it's built on. So what happens when that data disappears?

The AI race has quietly turned into a data protection problem, particularly with Agentic AI. Petabytes of training data, feature stores and analytics pipelines are sitting in cloud storage—most of it without a recovery plan that can keep up.

84% of enterprise leaders now run intentionally multi-cloud to power AI. But protection across those clouds is still mostly manual, slow and fragile.

I've been exploring this with Commvault, how their 𝘾𝙡𝙪𝙢𝙞𝙤 𝙩𝙚𝙖𝙢 𝙚𝙭𝙩𝙚𝙣𝙙𝙚𝙙 𝙎𝙖𝙖𝙎-𝙗𝙖𝙨𝙚𝙙 𝙧𝙖𝙥𝙞𝙙 𝙧𝙚𝙘𝙤𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙮 𝙩𝙤 𝙂𝙤𝙤𝙜𝙡𝙚 𝘾𝙡𝙤𝙪𝙙 𝙎𝙩𝙤𝙧𝙖𝙜𝙚 — immutable, air-gapped, and built for petabyte-scale AI & Analytics workloads.

𝗤𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗿:
🟥If your model training data was corrupted tomorrow, how long before you could rebuild?
🟧Are your AI pipelines protected at the same tier as your production databases?
🟨Who owns data resilience in an AI-first org — the data team, security, or platform?
🟩How is your team protecting the data layer behind your AI?
🟦Are you ready to bring Commvault's  Cyber/Data/Identity Resilience portfolio of solutions, products, services, training, and Cloud partnerships into your enterprise AI infrastructure & workflows?
🟪Clumio is ready for you. Are you ready for Clumio? 

🟣𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲: https://t.co/rbK3QD5Gt0
🟣𝗥𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗮 𝗗𝗲𝗺𝗼: https://t.co/8eLT99dQ6v

items.title: GPT with LangGraph! #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorc...
items.content:
GPT with LangGraph! #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode   

The rapid advancement of large model technology is leading to an increasing application of agent technology across various fields and industries significantly transforming how people work and live. In complex and dynamic environments, multi-agent systems are able to tackle intricate tasks that would be challenging for a single agent, thanks to their collaborative and division-of-labor approaches.   

The following stack of research papers and hands on tutorials highlight the integrated use of GPT with  LangGraph and CrewAI. LangGraph enhances information transmission efficiency through its graph-based structure, while CrewAI boosts team collaboration and system performance via intelligent task allocation and resource management. The key areas of this research include:   The design of agent architectures based on LangGraph for precise control . 

The enhancement of agent capabilities through CrewAI to tackle a range of tasks. The goal of this study is to explore the combined potential of GPT and LangGraph and CrewAI in multi-agent systems, offering fresh insights for the ongoing evolution of agent technology and fostering innovation in the application of large model intelligent agents.  References   Duan, Z., & Wang, J. (2024, November 27). Exploration of LLM multi-agent application implementation based on 

LangGraph+CrewAI. arXiv. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/pc2HNbyAm5 Horsey, J. (2025, March 9). Build a powerful Python chatbot in minutes with LangGraph. Geeky Gadgets. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/xpp0gyYZ5O Ong, R. (2024, July 10). GPT-4o and LangGraph tutorial: Build a TNT-LLM application. DataCamp. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/wzv4Bepqba Sivan, V. (2024). Building AI agent systems with LangGraph. Medium. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/oJO20UYuTV Wang, J., & Duan, Z. (2024, December 2). Intelligent Spark agents: A modular LangGraph framework for scalable, visualized, and enhanced big data machine learning workflows. arXiv. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/IONuxFWhPx

items.title:
The real shift is this:

We are moving from one-way messaging
to two-way, outcome-driven conversatio...
items.content:
The real shift is this:

We are moving from one-way messaging
to two-way, outcome-driven conversations.

That means a customer should be able to:

→ ask a question
→ get a real answer
→ change an appointment
→ buy a product
→ solve the issue

All inside the same thread.

Not across 5 channels.
Not after 3 handoffs.
Not 2 days later.

items.content:
MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT 

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month.

items.title: The contest is open to eligible participants who are 18 years of age or older. Employees, contractor...
items.content:
The contest is open to eligible participants who are 18 years of age or older. Employees, contractors, and immediate family members of OpenAI are not eligible to enter or win.

Winners will be selected from the pool of valid entries by eligible entrants. Each winner will receive one free ticket to OpenAI DevDay in San Francisco, California. Winners are responsible for all travel, lodging, meals, and other expenses not expressly included in the prize.

Winners will be notified by direct message and must respond by filling out a form by July 1, 2026. If a winner does not respond by the deadline, is ineligible, or cannot accept the prize, OpenAI may select an alternate winner.

No purchase necessary, entries must be submitted by July 1, 2026. Individuals located in Belarus, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Russia, Syria, Crimea, Sevastopol, and the non-government-controlled areas of Donetsk, Kherson, Luhansk, and Zaporizhzhia oblasts of Ukraine are not eligible to enter.

By entering, you agree that OpenAI may use submitted entries, and derivations thereof, in promotional material for OpenAI products, services, and events globally in any medium.

This contest and these terms are governed by the laws of the State of California, USA. Participation is also subject to the OpenAI Terms of Use, available at https://t.co/9Haa5QDP8d.

items.title:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning  

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical comp...
items.content:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning  

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical component of effective healthcare. Obesity is a significant risk factor for a range of serious diseases, including chronic conditions, type-2 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers. Understanding a person's obesity status can serve as a powerful catalyst for individuals to take control of their weight. Additionally, intentional weight management not only mitigates health risks but also offers the compelling benefit of reducing disease susceptibility.  Despite its widespread use, the Body Mass Index (BMI) – the standard metric defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) – fails to capture the complexities of obesity, as it overlooks essential body-type variations. Furthermore, nutritional needs differ markedly across regions and body types, underscoring the necessity for a more nuanced approach to obesity assessment.  Traditional anthropometric measurements, while effective, are often impractical due to the requirement for trained professionals to perform them accurately. In response to this challenge, innovative research utilizing 3D scanning technology is gaining momentum as a less-invasive and more accessible alternative. Unlike Computed Tomography (CT) or Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)—considered the gold standard for measuring body fat percentage (bf%)—3D scanners eliminate the risks associated with radiation exposure during frequent assessments.  Moreover, evaluating health risks demands a multifaceted approach rather than relying solely on a singular measure. In this study, we collected paired data from 3D body scans and DXA for a Korean population, providing a more comprehensive understanding of obesity and paving the way for improved health management strategies. This pioneering research has the potential to transform how we assess and respond to obesity, ultimately leading to healthier outcomes for individuals and communities alike.  

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode    

References  Jeon, S., Kim, M., Yoon, J., & et al. (2023). Machine learning-based obesity classification considering 3D body scanner measurements. Scientific Reports, 13, 3299. Retrieved February 27, 2025, from https://t.co/h3ZR1dCrCg
